feed.title: Word of the Day
feed.subtitle: Combined daily-word feed: Merriam-Webster, Dictionary.com, A.Word.A.Day, The Free Dictionary, Wiktionary, and the Collins language blog.

entry.title: itinerant — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 24, 2026 is: itinerant • \eye-TIN-uh-runt\ • adjective Itinerant describes someone who travels from place to place or only stays in a place for a short amount of time. It is used especially for people who travel in a circuit for their profession. // Being an itinerant musician gave her the opportunity to see the wonders of the world with her own eyes. See the entry > Examples: “‘There has long been this masculine idea that a lone, itinerant male blues
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: flagitious — The Free Dictionary
entry.content: Definition: (adjective) Shockingly brutal or cruel. Synonyms: atrocious , monstrous , grievous . Usage: All faults or defects, from the slightest misconduct to the most flagitious crime, Pantocyclus attributed to some deviation from perfect regularity in the bodily figure.
entry.dc:creator: The Free Dictionary

entry.title: echinate — A.Word.A.Day
entry.content: adjective: Spiny or bristly.
entry.dc:creator: A.Word.A.Day

entry.title: Argus-eyed — Dictionary.com
entry.content: adjective — extremely observant; watchful
entry.dc:creator: Dictionary.com

entry.title: union — Wiktionary
entry.content: union n ( countable ) The act of uniting or joining two or more things into one. ( uncountable ) The state of being united or joined ; a state of unity or harmony . ( countable ) Something united, or made one; something formed by a combination or coalition of parts or members; a confederation ; a consolidated body; a league . ( countable ) A trade union ; a workers' union. ( countable ) An association of students at a university for social and/or
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: maverick — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 23, 2026 is: maverick • \MAV-rik\ • noun Maverick refers to a person who refuses to follow the customs or rules of a group; in other words, a nonconformist . // Ayo has always been a bit of a maverick in the fashion world, inventing new trends rather than following them. See the entry > Examples: “ [Luis] Alvarez has been described as a scientific Indiana Jones, but his reputation as a maverick was built on a foundation of patience and discipline.” — A
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: deface — The Free Dictionary
entry.content: Definition: (verb) Mar or spoil the appearance of. Synonyms: blemish , disfigure . Usage: The vandals defaced the statue by drawing a mustache on it.
entry.dc:creator: The Free Dictionary

entry.title: bellwether — A.Word.A.Day
entry.content: noun: 1. An indicator of future trends. 2. A leader or trendsetter.
entry.dc:creator: A.Word.A.Day

entry.title: trencherman — Dictionary.com
entry.content: noun — a person with a hearty appetite
entry.dc:creator: Dictionary.com

entry.title: pyramidal — Wiktionary
entry.content: pyramidal adj ( geometry ) Pyramid -shaped. ( crystallography ) Tetragonal . Today is Revolution Day in Egypt , a public holiday in Egypt commemorating the Egyptian revolution of 1952 . ← yesterday | About Word of the Day Nominate a word Leave feedback | tomorrow →
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: French word of the week: effet — Collins
entry.content: Another Monday, another French blog. This week, we’ve chosen to explore the word effet in detail. We know that it … Continued
entry.dc:creator: Collins

entry.title: Spanish words of the week: fondo or fonda? — Collins
entry.content: It’s time to get to the bottom of some differences in usage between another two similar-looking Spanish words, gaining in-depth … Continued
entry.dc:creator: Collins

entry.title: stalwart — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 22, 2026 is: stalwart • \STAWL-wert\ • adjective Stalwart is a formal word used to describe someone or something that is very loyal and dedicated or that is physically or mentally strong. // He was a stalwart civil rights supporter throughout his political career. // The football team's stalwart defense only gave up a single field goal in the second half. See the entry > Examples: "Arbour is a stalwart defender of women's rights, having witnessed the s
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: billow — The Free Dictionary
entry.content: Definition: (verb) Rise up as if in waves. Synonyms: wallow . Usage: Smoke from the burning building billowed up into the sky, obscuring the sun.
entry.dc:creator: The Free Dictionary

entry.title: conspectus — Dictionary.com
entry.content: noun — a general overview
entry.dc:creator: Dictionary.com

entry.title: Boston — Wiktionary
entry.content: Boston marriage n ( historical ) A long-term monogamous relationship between two unmarried women, especially in New England between the 18th and early 20th centuries. On this day in 1893, Katharine Lee Bates , who was in a Boston marriage with Katharine Coman , summited Pikes Peak and was inspired to write “ America the Beautiful ”. ← yesterday | About Word of the Day Nominate a word Leave feedback | tomorrow →
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: dog days — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 21, 2026 is: dog days • \DAWG-DAYZ\ • plural noun Dog days is a plural noun that refers to the hottest time of the year, which in the northern hemisphere is usually between early July and early September. Dog days can also refer to a period of stagnation or inactivity suggestive of hot, sultry weather when it can be difficult to summon the energy required for hard work. // Spending the day at a swimming hole has always been the perfect relief for the d
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: epigram — The Free Dictionary
entry.content: Definition: (noun) A witty, often paradoxical remark, concisely expressed. Synonyms: quip . Usage: These gentlemen were obliged to be civil in public, yet they cut at each other with epigrams that were as sharp as razors.
entry.dc:creator: The Free Dictionary

entry.title: alack — Dictionary.com
entry.content: interjection — used as an exclamation of sorrow, regret, or dismay
entry.dc:creator: Dictionary.com

entry.title: deprecate — Wiktionary
entry.content: deprecate v ( transitive ) To express disapproval of, protest or plead against. ( transitive ) To belittle , depreciate . ( transitive , chiefly computing ) To declare something obsolescent ; to recommend against a function, technique, command, etc. that still works but has been replaced. ( archaic , transitive ) To pray against. ( archaic , transitive ) To regret deeply. On this day, 15 years ago, Space Shuttle Atlantis landed at Kennedy Space C
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: raffish — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 20, 2026 is: raffish • \RAF-ish\ • adjective Raffish describes people and things that show a careless but usually attractive unconventionality. // She didn't expect to like him but his raffish charm quickly won her over. See the entry > Examples: "Three or four decades ago, the newspaperman was appealingly raffish —at once a bum who drank too much and a knight-errant who charged unafraid at social injustice, succored the weak, and crossed lances with t
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: sapid — Dictionary.com
entry.content: adjective — having a strong pleasant flavor
entry.dc:creator: Dictionary.com

entry.title: scacchic — Wiktionary
entry.content: scacchic adj ( rare ) Of or relating to chess . Today is recognized by the United Nations as International Chess Day to note the role of the game in strengthening education, and promoting fairness, inclusion, and mutual respect. The Fédération Internationale des Échecs (International Chess Federation; FIDE ) was founded on this day in 1924. ← yesterday | About Word of the Day Nominate a word Leave feedback | tomorrow →
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: accentuate — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 19, 2026 is: accentuate • \ak-SEN-shu-wayt\ • verb To accentuate something is to make it more noticeable. // He likes to wear clothes that accentuate his muscular build. See the entry > Examples: “My mother could make me anything I wanted to be. As a child she transformed me into a swan, a mermaid, a white Persian cat dressed like an elegant Victorian lady with a porkpie hat and a ruffled bustle to accentuate my tail. She’d trained as a costume designe
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: hat trick — Dictionary.com
entry.content: noun — three goals scored by a single player in one game
entry.dc:creator: Dictionary.com

entry.title: whistle — Wiktionary
entry.content: whistle n A device designed to be placed in the mouth and blown, or driven by steam or some other mechanism, to make a whistling sound. An act of whistling. A shrill, high-pitched sound made by whistling. Any high-pitched sound similar to the sound made by whistling. ( Cockney rhyming slang ) A suit (from whistle and flute ). ( colloquial ) The mouth and throat; so called as being the organs of whistling. whistle v ( ambitransitive ) To make a sh
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: quid pro quo — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 18, 2026 is: quid pro quo • \kwid-proh-KWOH\ • noun Quid pro quo refers to something that is given to you or done for you in return for something you have given to or done for someone else. // If they do us this favor, they’ll expect a quid pro quo . See the entry > Examples: “For each proposal, it’s almost guaranteed you’ll have to negotiate with at least one faction, which usually involves some kind of quid pro quo . Maybe you agree to pass a policy
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: plurality — Wiktionary
entry.content: plurality n ( uncountable ) The state of being plural . ( ecclesiastical ) The holding of multiple benefices . ( countable ) A state of being numerous. ( countable ) A number or part of a whole which is greater than any other number or part, but not necessarily a majority. ( countable ) A number of votes for a single candidate or position which is greater than the number of votes gained by any other single candidate or position voted for, but whi
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: obdurate — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 17, 2026 is: obdurate • \AHB-duh-rut\ • adjective Obdurate is a formal word that means "resistant to persuasion." It is usually used to describe someone who is not willing to change their methods or opinions, or something that is not easily altered. // Though obdurate problems loom, the organization under its new leadership is poised to make real and effective changes. See the entry > Examples: "... even great careers must end sometime, and as often as
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: docking — Wiktionary
entry.content: docking n ( nautical ) The securing of a vessel to the quayside with cables . ( astronautics ) The process of connecting one spacecraft to another. The process of cutting off or trimming the tail or ears of an animal. ( molecular biology ) A method which predicts the preferred orientation of one molecule to a second when bound to each other to form a stable complex . docking v present participle and gerund of dock ( transitive verb ) Culinary ter
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: Elicit or Illicit? — Collins
entry.content: Elicit and illicit are homophones — words that sound the same but have different spellings and meanings. Even native speakers … Continued
entry.dc:creator: Collins

entry.title: Whether or If? — Collins
entry.content: One of the questions that learners often ask is: “What is the difference between whether and if?” It is a … Continued
entry.dc:creator: Collins

entry.title: bibelot — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 16, 2026 is: bibelot • \BEE-buh-loh\ • noun A bibelot is a small household ornament or decorative object. // His grandfather set up a table every week at the flea market where he sold all sorts of bibelots and bric-a-brac, from ceramic figurines to tiny blown glass sculptures. See the entry > Examples: “One by one, the beads and baubles and charms and bibelots have been cleaned and polished and now they are being placed on shelves, in jars and containe
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: glamour — Wiktionary
entry.content: glamour n ( uncountable ) Originally, enchantment ; magic charm ; especially, the effect of a spell that causes one to see objects in a form that differs from reality, typically to make filthy, ugly, or repulsive things seem beauteous . ( uncountable ) Alluring beauty or charm (often with sex appeal). ( uncountable ) Any excitement , appeal , or attractiveness associated with a person, place, or thing; that which makes something appealing . Any a
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: French word of the week: stage — Collins
entry.content: Welcome back to our blog! For this week’s vocabulary booster, we’ve chosen the word stage. As you might recognise this … Continued
entry.dc:creator: Collins

entry.title: Spanish word of the week: durar — Collins
entry.content: In this blog post it’s the enduring qualities of Spanish verb durar that we’re putting to the test: how you can use it and how it differs from tardar, another verb we’ve looked at recently and which takes a little time to separate out.
entry.dc:creator: Collins

entry.title: loquacious — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 15, 2026 is: loquacious • \loh-KWAY-shus\ • adjective Loquacious means “liking to talk and talking smoothly and easily.” Someone described as loquacious might also be called wordy (prone to using more words than necessary) or garrulous (tending to talk a lot). // We were entertained all afternoon by our host’s loquacious parrot and its nonstop script of favored phrases. See the entry > Examples: “Due to their loquacious nature, marmosets have recently
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster

entry.title: apprenticeship — Wiktionary
entry.content: apprenticeship n The condition of, or the time served by, an apprentice . The system by which a person learning a craft or trade is instructed by a master for a set time under set conditions . Today is observed by the United Nations as World Youth Skills Day in recognition of the importance of developing skills in the younger generation and preparing them for adulthood. ← yesterday | About Word of the Day Nominate a word Leave feedback | tomorrow
entry.dc:creator: Wiktionary

entry.title: yaw — Merriam-Webster
entry.content: Merriam-Webster's Word of the Day for July 14, 2026 is: yaw • \YAW\ • verb The verb yaw when used for the action of a ship, aircraft, spacecraft, or projectile means "to move to the left or right especially in an uncontrolled manner." More broadly, yaw is used as a synonym of alternate to mean "to change from one to another repeatedly." // The rogue wave hit the vessel, and the ship yawed hard to the right. // The album yaws from soulful ballad to up-tempo pop hit and back again. See the entry
entry.dc:creator: Merriam-Webster
